note: A seed corpus for the triage pass (see docs/triage.md and tools/triage.py). Each entry is a real-world harm scored by a human's structured reading, made reproducible. This is a SCOUT, not a judge: it ranks which wrongs are worth encoding as full scenarios next; it decides nothing. Precedents here are LEADS at institution level — the full cited-precedent verification the scenario schema demands happens at encode time, not triage time. reach and leverage are 1 (low) to 3 (high); see docs/triage.md for what each axis means.

cases.harm: Public contracts routed through a privileged lane; ministerially-referred firms won at roughly ten times the open rate.
cases.lead: R (Good Law Project & EveryDoctor) v SoS Health (High Court, 2022); NAO, PPE contracts (2022)

cases.harm: Information that exists is withheld by the holder running the clock: missed deadlines, self-granted extensions, requester profiling.
cases.lead: openDemocracy v Cabinet Office FOI Clearing House (FTT, 2021); R (Good Law Project) v SSHSC (reg 50, contract publication)

cases.harm: A regulator declines to enforce, and the forbearance is invisible because a non-decision produces nothing to appeal.
cases.lead: Office for Environmental Protection sewage-oversight findings (2023); Environment Agency prosecution gap

cases.harm: Government business run on private, auto-deleting messaging; the decision never enters any record, or is destroyed at source.
cases.lead: UK Covid-19 Inquiry (records not retained, some deliberately); Foxglove / Good Law Project

cases.harm: A documentary check at the polling station turns away voters who lack accepted photo ID, hitting the poor, disabled, young hardest.
cases.lead: Electoral Commission, Voter ID at the May 2023 local elections

cases.harm: Privileged access to ministers via informal lobbying and revolving-door appointments, invisible until reconstructed after the fact.
cases.lead: Greensill lobbying — Treasury Committee & Boardman Review (2021); ACOBA's lack of teeth

cases.harm: Political donations routed through opaque intermediaries (unincorporated associations) so the true source is never on the record.
cases.lead: Electoral Commission enforcement; PPERA 2000; unincorporated-association donation routes

cases.harm: An algorithm flags a claim as fraud-risk and support is suspended with no legible reason and no cited rule to appeal.
cases.lead: DWP algorithmic fraud-risk scoring; Carer's Allowance overpayments; Big Brother Watch reporting

cases.harm: A coercive act — detention or removal — carried out without an accessible, cited legal basis the subject can point to and challenge.
cases.lead: Immigration detention/removals litigation; the coercive.act warrant-citation analogue

cases.harm: A court rules a rape survivor's child must keep the rapist father's surname; the mother appeals under Article 8.
cases.lead: Good Law Project crowdfunder (the case that opened this thread)
cases.offstage_reason: A substantive judgment about a child's best interests under Article 8 — who is right. The kernel refuses to decide substance (refusal 11); this belongs to a court, and the protocol has no business weighing it.

cases.harm: A complaint that a doctor spread public-health misinformation and the GMC should act.
cases.lead: GMC professional-conduct complaint over public misinformation
cases.offstage_reason: Deciding whether a claim is misinformation is a judgment of truth — a ministry of truth is exactly what the kernel refuses to build (T11, refusal 11). Whether the GMC then acts is its substantive professional judgment.

cases.harm: A claim that a policy was simply the wrong call — unwise, not unlawful.
cases.lead: generic; recurs across the archive
cases.offstage_reason: Whether a policy is wise is a political judgment, not a process defect. There is no record to make, rule to cite, or pattern to surface — only a disagreement, which elections and argument settle, not infrastructure.

cases.harm: Sub-postmasters prosecuted and ruined on a faulty accounting system's output, trusted as evidence with no auditable trace anyone could challenge.
cases.lead: Bates v Post Office (2019, Fraser J); Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry (Sir Wyn Williams)

cases.harm: Patient records shared with third parties under weak consent, with no legible audit of who accessed what, or why.
cases.lead: care.data (scrapped 2016); NHS Federated Data Platform / Palantir; medConfidential

cases.harm: Police deploy live facial recognition on crowds with no accessible, cited basis a person can point to and challenge.
cases.lead: R (Bridges) v South Wales Police (Court of Appeal, 2020); Big Brother Watch

cases.harm: Councils unlawfully refuse or delay special-educational-needs assessments; each refusal defensible alone, the systemic pattern invisible.
cases.lead: Local Government & Social Care Ombudsman SEND reports; IPSEA

cases.harm: The ministerial code is judged by the prime minister alone; breaches the standards adviser finds go unacted-on, and advisers resign.
cases.lead: Resignations of successive Independent Advisers on Ministers' Interests (Allan 2020, Geidt 2022); PACAC

cases.harm: Police impose conditions or a ban on a protest under vague powers, the basis unrecorded and hard to contest before the day passes.
cases.lead: Public Order Act 2023; Liberty; protest-conditions litigation

cases.harm: A claim that a specific development — a tower, a green-belt release — should never have been approved.
cases.lead: generic; recurs across planning challenges
cases.offstage_reason: The merits of a planning decision are substantive and political — whether this was the right call — for the planning system and the courts, not a process defect a record can settle. The kernel can make the decision and its reasons legible; it cannot decide whether the decision was right.

cases.harm: Whether assisted dying should be lawful.
cases.lead: Terminally Ill Adults (End of Life) Bill 2024-25
cases.offstage_reason: A substantive moral and political question the kernel refuses on principle (refusal 11). It can run a fair, verifiable, receipt-free vote on the matter and hold the rights floor around it; it must never decide the answer — the line that keeps it from becoming a machine worth capturing.

cases.harm: An algorithm downgrades pupils' exam grades with no legible reason and no appeal, systematically favouring private schools.
cases.lead: Ofqual 2020 A-level algorithm (downgrades, then U-turn); Royal Statistical Society critique

cases.harm: Hospitals detain medically-cleared patients — and hold the bodies of the deceased — until unpaid bills are settled, with no lawful warrant and no route to contest the confinement.
cases.lead: Transnational Justice Initiative / 9BR Chambers; UN Working Group Against Arbitrary Detention (DRC debt-detention, 2025); via CrowdJustice — non-UK

cases.harm: Residents' and experts' fire-safety warnings are made repeatedly and ignored; the ignoring leaves no record to challenge until it is too late.
cases.lead: Grenfell Tower Inquiry; Grenfell Action Group warnings (pre-2017)

cases.harm: Police and prosecutors fail to disclose evidence to the defence; material that would clear the accused exists but is never handed over.
cases.lead: R v Liam Allan (2017, collapsed); HMCPSI / CJI disclosure reviews

cases.harm: A school inspection reduces everything to a single word with no legible reasoning and no timely appeal, with catastrophic consequences.
cases.lead: Ruth Perry inquest (2023); Ofsted single-word grades abolished (2024)

cases.harm: Citizenship is stripped by the Home Secretary on a national-security basis the person cannot fully see or contest in time.
cases.lead: British Nationality Act s.40; deprivation cases; closed-material procedure

cases.harm: Patients are given contaminated blood, not told for years, and the documents that would prove it are lost or destroyed.
cases.lead: Infected Blood Inquiry (Langstaff, final report 2024)

cases.harm: A dispute over the best interests of a critically ill child — whether to continue treatment.
cases.lead: Charlie Gard (2017); Archie Battersbee (2022)
cases.offstage_reason: The best interests of a critically ill patient are a substantive medical and ethical judgment for clinicians and a court, weighing evidence the protocol cannot and must not adjudicate. The kernel can make the decision and its reasons legible; it has no business deciding whether treatment should continue.

cases.harm: Whether a contested public statue should be removed.
cases.lead: Edward Colston statue, Bristol (2020)
cases.offstage_reason: Whether a monument should stand is a substantive question of values and history a community must argue and decide, not a process defect. The kernel can run a fair, verifiable vote on it and record the outcome; it must never decide the answer.

cases.harm: Carers unknowingly earn a few pounds over the Carer's Allowance limit; the DWP, holding the real-time earnings data that would flag it, says nothing for years, then claws back tens of thousands and prosecutes.
cases.lead: DWP Carer's Allowance overpayments (Guardian investigation 2024); NAO; Work and Pensions Committee

cases.harm: Repeated warnings, allegations and vetting flags about a serving officer are recorded and not acted on until he commits grave crimes; the failure to act leaves no reviewable trace.
cases.lead: Angiolini Inquiry (Couzens); David Carrick case; HMICFRS vetting super-complaint

cases.harm: Maternity units grade avoidable harm and deaths as low-risk to avoid scrutiny, and families raising concerns are not heard, so a pattern of failure stays invisible until an inquiry reconstructs it.
cases.lead: Ockenden Review (Shrewsbury & Telford); East Kent (Kirkup); Nottingham maternity review

cases.harm: Hundreds of thousands are convicted in private under the Single Justice Procedure — TV licence, DVLA, rail fares — with no hearing, no legible reasons, and often no real chance to respond.
cases.lead: Single Justice Procedure; Post Office SJP prosecutions; Transform Justice; Magistrates' Association concerns

cases.harm: The DWP conducts internal reviews into deaths of benefit claimants but withholds or heavily redacts them, so the pattern linking its decisions to deaths cannot be seen.
cases.lead: DWP Internal Process Reviews (FOI disclosures); Independent Case Examiner; NAO

cases.harm: Police seize cash and property from people never charged with a crime; to get it back the owner must prove the property's innocence, a burden many cannot meet.
cases.lead: US civil asset forfeiture; Institute for Justice 'Policing for Profit'; Timbs v Indiana (2019) — non-UK

cases.harm: Deciding whether a specific online post is harmful or illegal enough to remove, at platform scale, under threat of penalty.
cases.lead: Online Safety Act 2023; Ofcom illegal-harms duties
cases.offstage_reason: Deciding whether a particular post is harmful or unlawful is a judgment of truth and speech — the ministry of truth the kernel refuses to build (refusal 11, T11). The legibility of a takedown process (was a rule cited, is there an appeal) is a separate, onstage question; the merits are not the kernel's.

cases.harm: A court orders contact between a child and a parent; the order is breached serially; each enforcement application is heard alone, months later, and almost none ends in any sanction. No record aggregates the breaches, so the passage of time quietly becomes the ruling.
cases.lead: Fathers4Justice campaign territory (user-flagged); Family Justice Review; HMCTS enforcement statistics

cases.harm: Family courts decide childhoods in closed hearings: judgments largely unpublished, parties silenced by contempt rules, no way for anyone to check a pattern across cases — while the children's privacy that justifies the sealing is real, and cuts against the obvious fix.
cases.lead: Fathers4Justice campaign territory (user-flagged); President of the Family Division's Transparency Review (2021); reporting pilots from 2023

cases.harm: The campaign's central claim: that family-court contact and custody outcomes are systematically unjust to fathers, and the discretion itself is the wrong.
cases.lead: Fathers4Justice campaign territory (user-flagged)
cases.offstage_reason: A substantive judgment about a child's best interests and how judicial discretion should fall — who is right. The kernel refuses to decide substance (refusal 11), exactly as it refused the child-surname Article 8 case. What it can reach is the process around the judgment: the unpublished pattern (family-court-secrecy) and the unenforced order (family-contact-order-unenforced) — the two onstage halves of this campaign.

cases.harm: Corporate elections run through custodian chains nobody can verify end to end: a Delaware court found more facially valid vote claims than shares in existence, and proxy contests turn on recounts nobody outside the plumbing can check.
cases.lead: In re Dole Food Co. Stockholder Litigation (Del. Ch. 2017); Procter & Gamble/Trian proxy contest recount (2017)

cases.harm: Gig workers dismissed by automated decision with no reason served and nothing to appeal; an explanation took years and a foreign court order under data-protection law.
cases.lead: Drivers v Uber BV and Ola Cabs (Amsterdam Court of Appeal, 2023), GDPR art 22

cases.harm: Workplace recognition ballots run inside the employer's power — mandatory meetings, watched infrastructure, retaliation outside the booth — and a rerun arrives only after a regulator finds the interference, against a fear no rerun removes.
cases.lead: NLRB, Amazon Bessemer rerun order (2021); TULR(C)A Sch A1 recognition ballots (CAC)

cases.harm: Sellers suspended and apps delisted with no rule cited and revenue held; the EU had to legislate a statement of reasons because platforms gave none.
cases.lead: Platform-to-Business Regulation (EU) 2019/1150, arts 4-5; CMA, Mobile ecosystems market study (2022)

cases.harm: A mutual owned by millions ran its member democracy through tiers no member could follow or check; the structure was found manifestly dysfunctional only at the point of near-collapse.
cases.lead: Myners, Report of the Independent Governance Review, Co-operative Group (2014)

cases.harm: The meeting's only record is written by the side that won it — contemporaneous, signed, complete in form — and no other participant's account has anywhere to attach, so the archive is the victor's memo.
cases.lead: Butler Review (2004) on informal, unminuted decision-taking; Crossman, Diaries of a Cabinet Minister; Hillsborough Independent Panel (2012), 164 police statements altered; UK Covid-19 Inquiry Module 2 records evidence

cases.harm: When policy kills, inquiries burn years establishing what the minister was told and when: the advice exists on file, but its existence is as secret as its content, so 'nobody told me' and 'we advised against it' are both unfalsifiable.
cases.lead: Infected Blood Inquiry final report (2024); Post Office Horizon IT Inquiry, ministerial-knowledge phases; FOI Act 2000 s.35; Butler Review (2004)

cases.harm: A wrong is answered with process about process — a consultation, a review, an inquiry with no deadline — until the implicated have retired and the remedy is an apology to the dead.
cases.lead: Lords Statutory Inquiries Committee (2014; 2024 follow-up); Hillsborough (27 years); Infected Blood (harms from the 1970s, report 2024)
cases.covered_reason: The long grass is the clock family, already encoded four ways: the holder running the clock (foi-slow-walking), time quietly becoming the ruling (family-contact-order-unenforced), the review that learns nothing (dwp-death-reviews-secret), forbearance with no artifact (regulator-inaction), rationing by attrition (send-ehcp-refusals). The one new sliver — an inquiry owing its own deadline — is the timed-inbound-duty extension the corpus has already named and deferred. A sixth scenario would re-walk the five.

cases.harm: An inquiry is defused at birth by the choice of its chair and terms of reference: the appointment lawful, the candidate defensible, the effect a tribunal that will not bite.
cases.lead: IICSA chair churn (2014-16); Daniel Morgan Independent Panel; Inquiries Act 2005 s.10
cases.covered_reason: The checkable part — who appointed, on whose referral, under what terms — is the executive.act appointment with provenance the corpus already carries (revolving-door-lobbying, procurement-vip-lane), and the judge-is-the-defendant shape is ministerial-standards-unenforced. Whether the chair is the wrong chair is substance, refused (refusal 11).

cases.harm: The minister decides on a brief engineered to mislead — options pre-cooked, risks omitted, the courageous choice framed to die — and the decision is formally the minister's own.
cases.lead: Westland affair (1986); Butler Review (2004) on intelligence presented to the outer limits; generic across the archive
cases.offstage_reason: Whether advice was fair, complete or honest is a judgment of substance and truth — grading the quality of counsel is the ministry of truth refused outright (refusal 11). The kernel can make the advice-trail's existence checkable — who-told-the-minister carries that onstage sliver — but it must never mark a brief misleading, and the deeper harm, officials who stop writing candid advice at all, is a culture no schema field reaches.
